title: Death rate from venomous animal contact
subtitle: Annual number of deaths from venomous animal contact per 100,000 people. Venomous animals deliver these toxins as venom through a bite or sting.
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized).
caption:
Death rate from venomous animal contact

Annual number of deaths from venomous animal contact per 100,000 people. Venomous animals deliver these toxins as venom through a bite or sting.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 death rates from venomous animal contact per 100,000 people, using an age-standardized metric to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. The legend ranges from 0 to 5, with specific tick points at 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5. The darkest red shades, indicating rates above 2 and extending toward 5, are concentrated in specific regions: Madagascar, Guyana, and parts of West Africa including Mali and Burkina Faso fall into these highest visible bands. Surrounding countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, such as Nigeria, Cameroon, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, appear in mid-to-dark red shades that sit between the 1 and 2 thresholds. In South Asia, India and Bangladesh show darker red coloring that places them in the 1 to 2 range, while neighboring Pakistan and Myanmar sit in slightly lighter bands. Southeast Asian nations like Indonesia and the Philippines display mid-range orange shades between 0.5 and 1. Latin America shows variation, with Brazil and Colombia in lighter orange tones near 0.2 to 0.5, while Guyana stands out in the darkest band. North America, most of Europe, Australia, and Russia sit predominantly in the lightest shades of the legend, near or below 0.1. Greenland and a few small island territories show hatching, indicating no data. The visual pattern shows the highest rates concentrated in parts of Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and isolated countries in South America and the Indian Ocean, with the lowest rates distributed across much of North America, Europe, and Oceania.
